summary: I come from Nagano Prefecture, also known as Shinshu. It was there that my father worked as a school teacher and my family had roots in Shinto priesthood. The prefecture supported students like me to study abroad, and I took a test to attend Dobun Shoin in Shanghai. Nagano shaped much of my early life and education before I left for America in 1907.

Shinshu is another name for Nagano Prefecture, where I was born and raised. My family was involved in Shinto priesthood there, and my father also taught at a school. Growing up in Shinshu, I experienced the local customs and education system before moving abroad. It was a place with rich traditions and a strong connection to Shinto practices.

My family were Shinto priests for generations, including my father and mother. Though they couldn't make a living solely from priesthood, they also farmed. I grew up reciting Shinto ritual prayers before meals and on special days, and our home had a shrine with charms from the Grand Shrine of Ise. Even after becoming a Christian, I reflected on the role of priests like Hiraga Genshin in my family lineage.

Hachimantaro Yoshiiye was the god of our family shrine. His body is enshrined at the Hachiman Shrine in Kamakura, and part of his body was brought to our local shrine. We held him in high regard as a protective deity connected to our family's spiritual heritage. His presence linked our small shrine to a greater religious tradition in Japan.

The Hachiman Shrine in Kamakura is where the body of Hachimantaro Yoshiiye is enshrined. Our family shrine holds a part of his body, which connects us to that historic place. This shrine represents an important spiritual link for my family, rooted in the traditions of Shinto and the veneration of Hachimantaro Yoshiiye.

Kamakura is where the Hachiman Shrine is located, which enshrines the body of Hachimantaro Yoshiiye, our family’s god. Part of his body was brought from Kamakura to our local shrine, creating a spiritual connection between our home and that historic city. Kamakura thus holds significance for my family's religious heritage.

Hiraga Genshin was a retainer of Takeda Shingen and an ancestor of my family. He later became a priest, and after I became a Christian, I thought of him as similar to a priest in the Bible. His story connects my family history to the samurai era and religious service in Japan.

Takeda Shingen was a famous warlord during the Civil War period, and Hiraga Genshin, my ancestor, was one of his retainers. This connection to Takeda Shingen places my family in the historical context of Japan’s feudal past. It is part of the legacy I grew up knowing about.

Inside our house, we enshrined a charm from the Grand Shrine of Ise, which is one of the most important Shinto shrines in Japan. This charm was part of our daily religious practice, reflecting the belief in many gods in Japan. It was especially significant during New Year's, when we expected the god of New Year to visit.

Near our home was a temple where Buddhist priests recited prayers early in the morning on the 1st and 7th of the month. I could hear their drums, but to me, these were just rituals, and I often prayed to unknown gods. Buddhism was part of the religious landscape around me, alongside Shinto practices.

After the Russo-Japanese War, Japanese soldiers brought back hard bread, and I remember eating some with other teachers when we were drunk after a victory party. It was a time when sake was served at celebrations, and we had to apologize to village elders for our behavior. The war's aftermath was part of the social fabric in my village.

The Dobun Shoin in Shanghai was a school that two students from Nagano Prefecture could attend with expenses paid by the prefecture. I took the exam to enter Dobun Shoin but did not pass. This opportunity influenced my decision to come to America instead in 1907.

Shanghai was home to the Dobun Shoin, a school where some students from Nagano Prefecture could study with financial support. I tried to pass the entrance exam to attend but failed, which led me to come to America instead. Shanghai represented an alternative path for education at that time.

I came to America in November 1907, hoping for better opportunities and thinking money grew on trees. I landed in Seattle and initially wanted to go to Alaska for gold, but was warned against it. America was a place of hope and challenge, where I worked various jobs and eventually joined the Methodist Episcopal Church.

When I first came to America, I wanted to go to Alaska to find gold nuggets and make money to study. However, a man who had been there warned me not to go because many became 'headless'—losing all their earnings gambling on the way back. So I stayed in Seattle instead and did not make the trip to Alaska.

Seattle was where I landed when I came to America in 1907. I stayed at a rooming house managed by the Methodist Episcopal Church, which helped me find work. I lived there until the war broke out, working on ranches, picking hops, and sometimes on the Northern Pacific and Great Northern Railways.

My ship to America left from Yokohama, where I underwent an eye examination for trachoma before departure. I passed the test after receiving treatment in my hometown and Takasaki. Yokohama was the last place I was in Japan before starting my journey to America.

Before leaving Japan, I went to Takasaki for treatment of epidemic ophthalmia to prepare for the eye examination required at Yokohama. This treatment helped me pass the medical check before boarding the ship to America. Takasaki was part of my health preparations for the journey abroad.

When I arrived in Seattle, I stayed at a rooming house managed by the Methodist Episcopal Church. Although I initially went to church just to find a job, the pastor and elders encouraged me to be baptized, and I became a Christian. The church played a crucial role in my life by providing support and spiritual guidance far from home.

Some Japanese immigrants, including myself, worked as laborers on the Northern Pacific Railway near Seattle. This was one of the common jobs for those who did not work in canneries or farms. The railway work was part of the labor opportunities available to us in America before the war.

Alongside the Northern Pacific, the Great Northern Railway also employed Japanese laborers like me around Seattle. Working on these railways was typical for immigrants seeking jobs in America in the early 1900s. It was hard work but a way to earn a living while adjusting to life in a new country.
The transcript does not mention Vashon Island, so I have no memories or experiences to share about it.
